title: How to disable podman from managing it's own firewalld rules
body:
Hi

Is there a way to stop podman from managing it's own firewall rules?  Podman is putting all of the container's open ports in trusted  zone of firewalld and I can't find a way to limit access to a set of ip addresses only over the internet.

Ideally I think podman should configure it's own zone rather than use the built-in trusted zone.

comments.body: With Podman v4.0, we are looking into implementing this via the new Netavark network stack, but it will not be available in the initial release due to missing features in firewalld (https://github.com/firewalld/firewalld/pull/903 which will arrive in Firewalld 1.1.0). Using the present stack, you can theoretically disable the firewalld code by removing the `firewall` plugin from your CNI configurations, but no traffic will pass through the firewall without it (unless you manually add rules to do so)
I'll go ahead and close since this is resolved, and we're just waiting on upstream releases.
Thanks for the quick response. Would I still need to use iptables if I managed it myself?
also how do you remove plugin via command line or do you hva eto manually edit the conlist file ?

You'd have to manually edit the conflist.

Removing the firewall plugin will prevent the firewalld rules from being added, but the normal iptables rules used by Podman would still be created. Unfortunately, firewalld will deny traffic from these rules unless you allow the relevant subnets through firewalld.

my thought is to create my own zone called podman, set target to DROP and then add rich-rules for only the ip addresses  that must access the containers.

If I force the ip address of the containers in docker-compose then I will know their ip address to add to the sources section.  Would this work or will it just be the same as if it was in trusted zone?

I think that should work, but I've never tried it myself - the way we're doing it in Netavark has our subnets in a separate zone, but set to ALLOW, with firewalld handling NAT and port forwarding, and no iptables rules.
